[{"review_id": "reviewid_135", "user_id": "userid_548", "business_ref": "businessref_34", "rating": "2", "useful": "0", "funny": "0", "cool": "0", "text": "Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.", "date": "August 01, 2016 at 03:44 AM"}, {"review_id": "reviewid_1067", "user_id": "userid_213", "business_ref": "businessref_89", "rating": "5", "useful": "2", "funny": "0", "cool": "0", "text": "Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. var_functions.query_db:8.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

Terrible service. I was charged twice for online order and they refused to refund me. Numerous times this has happened

args.code:
import json
import pandas as pd

# Load the data from storage
business_data = var_functions.query_db:10
review_data = var_functions.query_db:12

# Read the full data from the files (they are file paths)
with open(business_data, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

with open(review_data, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
df_business = pd.DataFrame(businesses)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews)

# Standardize the IDs by removing prefixes
df_business['business_id_clean'] = df_business['business_id'].str.replace('businessid_', '')
df_reviews['business_id_clean'] = df_reviews['business_ref'].str.replace('businessref_', '')

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
credit_card_businesses = []
for idx, row in df_business.iterrows():
    attrs = row['attributes']
    accepts_cards = False
    if isinstance(attrs, dict):
        card_attr = attrs.get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards')
        if card_attr and str(card_attr).lower() == 'true':
            accepts_cards = True
    credit_card_businesses.append(accepts_cards)

df_business['accepts_credit_cards'] = credit_card_businesses

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
df_cc_businesses = df_business[df_business['accepts_credit_cards']].copy()

# Extract categories from description
import re

def extract_categories(description):
    if not description or pd.isna(description):
        return []
    
    # Look for patterns like "services in X, Y, and Z" or "category of 'X, Y'"
    categories = []
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in ..." followed by a list
    match1 = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|, providing|$)', description, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match1:
        text = match1.group(1)
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 2: "category of '...'"
    match2 = re.search(r"category of '(.+?)'", description, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:
        text = match2.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 3: "offers ... in the fields of ..."
    match3 = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|, making|$)', description, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match3:
        text = match3.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Clean up
    categories = [cat for cat in categories if cat and len(cat) > 2]
    return categories

df_cc_businesses['categories'] = df_cc_businesses['description'].apply(extract_categories)

# Explode categories and count
category_counts = {}
category_business_ids = {}

for idx, row in df_cc_businesses.iterrows():
    bid = row['business_id_clean']
    cats = row['categories']
    for cat in cats:
        if cat not in category_counts:
            category_counts[cat] = 0
            category_business_ids[cat] = []
        category_counts[cat] += 1
        category_business_ids[cat].append(bid)

# Find categories with most businesses
sorted_categories = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(sorted_categories[:10], indent=2))

    if match1:
        text = match1.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])

    match2 = re.search(r"category of '(.+?)'", description, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match2:

df_reviews['business_id_clean'] = df_reviews['business_ref'].str.replace('businessref_', '')

# Filter businesses that accept credit cards
accepts_cc = []
for _, row in df_business.iterrows():
    attrs = row['attributes']
    is_cc = False
    if isinstance(attrs, dict) and attrs.get('BusinessAcceptsCreditCards'):
        if str(attrs['BusinessAcceptsCreditCards']).lower() == 'true':
            is_cc = True
    accepts_cc.append(is_cc)

df_business['accepts_cc'] = accepts_cc
cc_businesses = df_business[df_business['accepts_cc']].copy()

# Extract categories from descriptions
def get_categories(desc):
    if not desc or pd.isna(desc):
        return []
    cats = []
    
    # Pattern for "services in A, B, and C"
    m1 = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|, providing|$)', desc, re.I)
    if m1:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', m1.group(1))
        cats.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern for "category of 'X, Y'"
    m2 = re.search(r"category of '(.+?)'", desc, re.I)
    if m2:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', m2.group(1))
        cats.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern for "fields of X, Y"
    m3 = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|, making|$)', desc, re.I)
    if m3:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', m3.group(1))
        cats.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    return [c for c in cats if len(c) > 2]

cc_businesses['categories'] = cc_businesses['description'].apply(get_categories)

# Count businesses per category
cat_counts = {}
cat_biz_ids = {}

for _, row in cc_businesses.iterrows():
    biz_id = row['business_id_clean']
    for cat in row['categories']:
        cat_counts[cat] = cat_counts.get(cat, 0) + 1
        if cat not in cat_biz_ids:
            cat_biz_ids[cat] = []
        cat_biz_ids[cat].append(biz_id)

# Find top category
if cat_counts:
    top_cat = max(cat_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    top_cat_name = top_cat[0]
    top_cat_count = top_cat[1]
    
    # Get ratings for businesses in top category
    top_biz_ids = cat_biz_ids[top_cat_name]
    top_ratings = df_reviews[df_reviews['business_id_clean'].isin(top_biz_ids)]['rating'].astype(float)
    avg_rating = top_ratings.mean() if len(top_ratings) > 0 else 0
    
    result = {
        'top_category': top_cat_name,
        'business_count': int(top_cat_count),
        'average_rating': round(float(avg_rating), 2)
    }
else:
    result = {'top_category': None, 'business_count': 0, 'average_rating': 0}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:8.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import re

# Access the file path from storage
business_file_path = var_functions.query_db_26

# Read the credit card accepting businesses
with open(business_file_path, 'r') as f:
    cc_businesses = json.load(f)

# Process to extract categories
category_counts = {}
cat_biz_ids = {}

for biz in cc_businesses:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    biz_id = biz['business_id'].replace('businessid_', '')
    
    # Extract categories using regex patterns
    categories = []
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in ..."
    m1 = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|, providing|$)', desc, re.I)
    if m1:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', m1.group(1))
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 2: "category of '...'"
    m2 = re.search(r"category of '(.+?)'", desc, re.I)
    if m2:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', m2.group(1))
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 3: "fields of ..."
    m3 = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|, making|$)', desc, re.I)
    if m3:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', m3.group(1))
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    for cat in categories:
        if len(cat) > 2:
            category_counts[cat] = category_counts.get(cat, 0) + 1
            if cat not in cat_biz_ids:
                cat_biz_ids[cat] = []
            cat_biz_ids[cat].append(biz_id)

# Find top category
if category_counts:
    top_cat, top_count = max(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    top_category_business_ids = cat_biz_ids[top_cat]
else:
    top_cat, top_count = None, 0
    top_category_business_ids = []

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_category': top_cat,
    'business_count': top_count,
    'business_ids_sample': top_category_business_ids[:5],
    'total_unique_categories': len(category_counts)
}))

val_args.code:
import json
import re

# Access the file path from storage
business_file_path = var_functions.query_db_26

# Read the credit card accepting businesses
with open(business_file_path, 'r') as f:
    cc_businesses = json.load(f)

# Process to extract categories
category_counts = {}
cat_biz_ids = {}

for biz in cc_businesses:
    desc = biz.get('description', '')
    if not desc:
        continue
    
    biz_id = biz['business_id'].replace('businessid_', '')
    
    # Extract categories using regex patterns
    categories = []
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in ..."
    m1 = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|, providing|$)', desc, re.I)
    if m1:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', m1.group(1))
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 2: "category of '...'"
    m2 = re.search(r"category of '(.+?)'", desc, re.I)
    if m2:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', m2.group(1))
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 3: "fields of ..."
    m3 = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|, making|$)', desc, re.I)
    if m3:
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', m3.group(1))
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    for cat in categories:
        if len(cat) > 2:
            category_counts[cat] = category_counts.get(cat, 0) + 1
            if cat not in cat_biz_ids:
                cat_biz_ids[cat] = []
            cat_biz_ids[cat].append(biz_id)

# Find top category
if category_counts:
    top_cat, top_count = max(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    top_category_business_ids = cat_biz_ids[top_cat]
else:
    top_cat, top_count = None, 0
    top_category_business_ids = []

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'top_category': top_cat,
    'business_count': top_count,
    'business_ids_sample': top_category_business_ids[:5],
    'total_unique_categories': len(category_counts)
}))

var_functions.query_db:8.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
import pandas as pd
import re

# Read the credit card accepting businesses
cc_business_file = var_functions.query_db_36
with open(cc_business_file, 'r') as f:
    cc_businesses = json.load(f)

# Read all reviews
review_file = var_functions.query_db_12
with open(review_file, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Convert to DataFrames
df_cc = pd.DataFrame(cc_businesses)
df_reviews = pd.DataFrame(reviews)

# Extract clean business IDs
df_cc['business_id_clean'] = df_cc['business_id'].str.replace('businessid_', '')
df_reviews['business_id_clean'] = df_reviews['business_ref'].str.replace('businessref_', '')

# Extract categories from descriptions
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description or pd.isna(description):
        return []
    
    categories = []
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in ..." 
    m1 = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|, providing|$)', description, re.I)
    if m1:
        text = m1.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 2: "category of '...'"
    m2 = re.search(r"category of '(.+?)'", description, re.I)
    if m2:
        text = m2.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 3: "fields of ..."
    m3 = re.search(r'fields of (.+?)(?:\.|, making|$)', description, re.I)
    if m3:
        text = m3.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Pattern 4: "in the categories of ..." or "categories of ..."
    m4 = re.search(r'categories?(?: of)? (.+?)(?:\.|, offering|$)', description, re.I)
    if m4:
        text = m4.group(1)
        parts = re.split(r',\s*', text)
        categories.extend([p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip()])
    
    # Clean up
    return [cat for cat in categories if len(cat) > 2]

df_cc['categories'] = df_cc['description'].apply(extract_categories)

# Count businesses per category
from collections import defaultdict

category_counts = defaultdict(int)
category_biz_ids = defaultdict(list)

for idx, row in df_cc.iterrows():
    biz_id = row['business_id_clean']
    for cat in row['categories']:
        category_counts[cat] += 1
        category_biz_ids[cat].append(biz_id)

# Find top category
if category_counts:
    top_category = max(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
    top_category_name = top_category[0]
    top_category_count = top_category[1]
    top_biz_ids = category_biz_ids[top_category_name]
    
    # Calculate average rating for top category
    df_top_ratings = df_reviews[df_reviews['business_id_clean'].isin(top_biz_ids)]
    avg_rating = df_top_ratings['rating'].astype(float).mean()
    
    # Also get some stats
    total_reviews = len(df_top_ratings)
    unique_businesses = len(set(top_biz_ids))
    
    result = {
        'top_category': top_category_name,
        'business_count': int(top_category_count),
        'average_rating': round(float(avg_rating), 2) if not pd.isna(avg_rating) else 0.0,
        'total_reviews': int(total_reviews),
        'unique_businesses_rated': int(unique_businesses)
    }
else:
    result = {
        'top_category': None,
        'business_count': 0,
        'average_rating': 0.0,
        'total_reviews': 0,
        'unique_businesses_rated': 0
    }

# Print result
print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!
